_meta.cycle_disposition_reason: Weekly research bundle contains regime-level structural developments: USTR Section 301 legal architecture shift, Fed and ECB stagflation constraint binding, IMF global growth downgrade with energy shock scenarios. Three SA signals across multiple domains exceed materiality threshold.

lead_signal.headline: USTR launches 76 Section 301 investigations as durable legal backstop for tariff regime following Supreme Court IEEPA nullification
lead_signal.body: The United States Trade Representative initiated 76 new Section 301 investigations — 60 targeting inadequate foreign enforcement against forced labor and 16 addressing structural excess manufacturing capacity — as a permanent legal replacement for IEEPA-based tariffs struck down by the Supreme Court in February 2026. Section 301 is a statutory trade retaliation authority historically resistant to judicial challenge, representing a structural hardening of the tariff regime rather than tactical escalation. The Balance of Payments interim 10 percent blanket tariff under Section 122 serves as a bridge pending Section 301 outcomes, with the 150-day BOP clock expiring mid-July 2026. This legal re-architecture signals the administration intends to maintain tariff leverage as a permanent structural tool, not a negotiating chip to be retired post-bilateral framework agreements.

module_0.title: USTR Section 301 Legal Architecture: Tariff Regime Structural Hardening Post-SCOTUS IEEPA Nullification
module_0.body: The USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations this week — 60 for forced labor enforcement gaps and 16 for excess capacity — as a durable statutory replacement for IEEPA-based tariffs nullified by the Supreme Court in February 2026. Section 301 is historically harder to challenge in court than IEEPA, making this a regime-level legal shift rather than a tactical tariff announcement. The BOP interim 10 percent tariff (Section 122) bridges to Section 301 outcomes, with a hard mid-July 2026 expiry. Market pricing of tariff de-escalation risk may be premature given the legal architecture being constructed. This development affects six asset classes directly: Bonds, Tech, Energy, Metals, EM Equities, Consumer Staples, and Real Estate.

mainstream.headline: FOMC holds rates at 3.5 to 3.75 percent; stagflation constraint binding
mainstream.why_it_matters: The Federal Reserve voted unanimously on April 29, 2026 to maintain the federal funds rate target at 3.5 to 3.75 percent with no forward guidance shift. The hold reflects the Fed dual-constraint bind: tariff-driven inflation (PIIE models show plus 1 percentage point above baseline) prevents cuts, while tariff-induced growth drag (PIIE: minus 0.62 percentage point from baseline in 2026) prevents hikes. The Fed is effectively frozen in a stagflationary corridor, with the Middle East energy shock adding a second inflationary vector. This represents a regime-level policy constraint, not a cyclical pause.
mainstream.body: The Federal Reserve voted unanimously on April 29, 2026 to maintain the federal funds rate target at 3.5 to 3.75 percent with no forward guidance shift. The hold reflects the Fed dual-constraint bind: tariff-driven inflation (PIIE models show plus 1 percentage point above baseline) prevents cuts, while tariff-induced growth drag (PIIE: minus 0.62 percentage point from baseline in 2026) prevents hikes. The Fed is effectively frozen in a stagflationary corridor, with the Middle East energy shock adding a second inflationary vector. This represents a regime-level policy constraint, not a cyclical pause.
mainstream.asymmetric: Fed funds futures may be pricing 1 to 2 cuts in H2 2026. GMM assessment: with Section 301 investigations likely to produce new tariff rounds and Middle East energy shock sustaining inflation, the bar for cuts is higher than market pricing implies. Asymmetric risk toward extended hold or hike.

mainstream.headline: ECB holds rates; flags intensified stagflation risk from Middle East war
mainstream.why_it_matters: The ECB Governing Council held all three key rates unchanged on April 30, 2026, but materially upgraded its risk language: upside inflation risks and downside growth risks have intensified, explicitly attributing this to the Middle East war energy price shock. The ECB noted that the longer the war continues and energy prices remain elevated, the stronger the impact on broader inflation and the economy. This represents a hawkish hold — the ECB is signaling that the easing cycle that began in 2024 is now suspended indefinitely.
mainstream.body: The ECB Governing Council held all three key rates unchanged on April 30, 2026, but materially upgraded its risk language: upside inflation risks and downside growth risks have intensified, explicitly attributing this to the Middle East war energy price shock. The ECB noted that the longer the war continues and energy prices remain elevated, the stronger the impact on broader inflation and the economy. This represents a hawkish hold — the ECB is signaling that the easing cycle that began in 2024 is now suspended indefinitely.
mainstream.asymmetric: EUR rate markets may still be pricing residual ECB cuts in 2026. GMM assessment: ECB language now explicitly links energy shock duration to inflation persistence — the easing path is closed unless the Middle East conflict de-escalates rapidly. Market pricing residual cuts; GMM assesses extended hold.

mainstream.headline: IMF April 2026 WEO cuts global growth to 3.1 percent, raises inflation to 4.4 percent; Middle East war dominant shock
mainstream.why_it_matters: The IMF April 2026 World Economic Outlook downgraded global growth to 3.1 percent for 2026 and raised headline inflation to 4.4 percent, with the Middle East war identified as the dominant shock via a 19 percent energy price rise in the reference scenario. The IMF adverse scenario (further disruption) projects growth falling to 2.5 percent and inflation rising to 5.4 percent; the severe scenario (energy disruption extending into 2027) projects growth at 2.0 percent and inflation exceeding 6.0 percent. EM sovereign spreads are flagged as vulnerable, with capital flows increasingly skewed toward debt over FDI.
mainstream.body: The IMF April 2026 World Economic Outlook downgraded global growth to 3.1 percent for 2026 and raised headline inflation to 4.4 percent, with the Middle East war identified as the dominant shock via a 19 percent energy price rise in the reference scenario. The IMF adverse scenario (further disruption) projects growth falling to 2.5 percent and inflation rising to 5.4 percent; the severe scenario (energy disruption extending into 2027) projects growth at 2.0 percent and inflation exceeding 6.0 percent. EM sovereign spreads are flagged as vulnerable, with capital flows increasingly skewed toward debt over FDI.
mainstream.asymmetric: Consensus may be anchoring to the IMF reference scenario (3.1 percent growth). GMM assessment: the adverse and severe scenarios are not tail risks but plausible paths given Hormuz fragility (5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal) and no credible ceasefire mechanism. Market pricing may underweight the probability of the adverse scenario materializing.

underweighted.headline: Section 301 legal architecture creates durable tariff baseline; market mispricing BOP expiry as de-escalation
underweighted.why_it_matters: The USTR Section 301 investigations represent a structural legal shift to a more challenge-resistant statutory basis for tariffs, not a tactical escalation. The BOP interim 10 percent tariff expires mid-July 2026, but Section 301 outcomes will replace it with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent in targeted areas (forced labor, excess capacity). Market pricing of bilateral framework agreements (UK, EU, Japan, Indonesia, Argentina, Ecuador) as de-escalation signals may be premature — the administration is constructing a permanent high-tariff architecture, not negotiating away leverage.
underweighted.body: The USTR Section 301 investigations represent a structural legal shift to a more challenge-resistant statutory basis for tariffs, not a tactical escalation. The BOP interim 10 percent tariff expires mid-July 2026, but Section 301 outcomes will replace it with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent in targeted areas (forced labor, excess capacity). Market pricing of bilateral framework agreements (UK, EU, Japan, Indonesia, Argentina, Ecuador) as de-escalation signals may be premature — the administration is constructing a permanent high-tariff architecture, not negotiating away leverage.
underweighted.asymmetric: Consensus narrative: bilateral deals signal tariff de-escalation path. GMM assessment: Section 301 architecture signals tariff leverage as permanent structural tool. Effective tariff rate baseline likely to rise post-BOP expiry, not fall. Divergence: market pricing de-escalation; GMM assesses structural entrenchment.

underweighted.headline: US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars plus 1 trillion dollars annual interest meets tariff-fueled inflation; fiscal dominance risk rising
underweighted.why_it_matters: The US faces a 9.8 trillion dollar Treasury maturity wall over the next 24 months, with annual interest expense exceeding 1 trillion dollars. Tariff-driven inflation (PIIE: plus 1 percentage point above baseline) and Middle East energy shock inflation create a dual vector that constrains Fed easing and raises refinancing costs. If the Fed is forced to hold or hike rates to combat inflation while the Treasury must roll over 9.8 trillion dollars, the risk of fiscal dominance (Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis) rises materially. This is a non-consensus tail risk underweighted by markets focused on the Iran war.
underweighted.body: The US faces a 9.8 trillion dollar Treasury maturity wall over the next 24 months, with annual interest expense exceeding 1 trillion dollars. Tariff-driven inflation (PIIE: plus 1 percentage point above baseline) and Middle East energy shock inflation create a dual vector that constrains Fed easing and raises refinancing costs. If the Fed is forced to hold or hike rates to combat inflation while the Treasury must roll over 9.8 trillion dollars, the risk of fiscal dominance (Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis) rises materially. This is a non-consensus tail risk underweighted by markets focused on the Iran war.
underweighted.asymmetric: Consensus focus: Middle East war and energy shock. GMM assessment: the intersection of tariff inflation, energy inflation, and the Treasury maturity wall creates a fiscal dominance scenario that is structurally more dangerous than a transient energy shock. Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide a cushion, but the cushion is thinning. Non-consensus tail risk with high impact if triggered.

flow_events.headline: IMF flags EM capital flows increasingly skewed toward debt over FDI, raising refinancing risk
flow_events.asymmetric: Debt-heavy flows increase EM sovereign vulnerability to Fed hold and dollar strength. Structural shift from equity to debt financing raises rollover risk.

flow_events.headline: BIS USD debt loop in EM economies flagged as structural vulnerability amid rising dollar funding costs
flow_events.asymmetric: EM economies with high USD-denominated debt face refinancing stress as Fed holds rates and dollar strengthens. Structural loop amplifies sovereign spread widening.

liquidity_conditions.headline: FX swap basis partial normalization on ceasefire news is episodic, not structural; volumes remain at all-time high 3.6 trillion dollars notional
liquidity_conditions.asymmetric: Reasoner flags fx swap basis normalization as TS episodic noise, not SA movement. Structural dollar funding stress persists despite tactical easing.

liquidity_conditions.headline: Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide cushion, but cushion thinning amid Treasury maturity wall
liquidity_conditions.asymmetric: Reserves ample but declining trajectory raises liquidity fragmentation risk if Treasury issuance accelerates to meet 9.8 trillion dollar maturity wall.

reserve_movements.asymmetric: Gold reserve ratio in EM central banks rising as dollar weaponization concerns persist. Structural shift away from USD reserves continues.

currency_signals.headline: Dollar safe-haven bid persists; EM FX under pressure from Fed hold and growth downgrade

major_moves.driver: Fed hold at 3.5 to 3.75 percent and risk-off sentiment sustain dollar strength
major_moves.regime_implication: Dollar strength amplifies EM sovereign stress via USD-denominated debt refinancing costs
major_moves.asymmetric: Market may be underpricing duration of dollar strength given Fed stagflation bind and Section 301 tariff durability

major_moves.driver: Fed hold, dollar strength, IMF EM growth downgrade to 3.9 percent, and capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI
major_moves.regime_implication: EM FX depreciation raises import inflation and sovereign refinancing stress
major_moves.asymmetric: BIS USD debt loop amplifies EM FX stress. Structural vulnerability, not cyclical weakness.

currency_signals.fx_stress_flags: EM FX stress is highest this week, driven by the intersection of Fed hold, dollar strength, IMF growth downgrade to 3.9 percent, and capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI. The BIS USD debt loop creates a structural amplification mechanism: EM economies with high USD-denominated debt face rising refinancing costs as the dollar strengthens, which forces further FX depreciation, which raises import inflation and sovereign stress. This is a structural loop, not a cyclical adjustment.
currency_signals.dollar_dynamics: The dollar remains in a safe-haven bid driven by the Fed stagflation bind (unable to cut due to tariff and energy inflation, unable to hike due to growth drag) and risk-off sentiment from the Middle East war. The Section 301 tariff architecture shift reinforces dollar strength by signaling a permanent high-tariff regime that reduces global trade and capital flows, increasing demand for dollar liquidity. Market pricing of Fed cuts in H2 2026 may be premature given the dual inflation vectors (tariffs and energy) and the Treasury maturity wall refinancing pressure. Dollar strength transmits globally via EM sovereign stress, commodity price suppression, and financial tightening.

sovereign_debt.headline: EM sovereign spreads vulnerable; US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars meets tariff inflation

spread_movements.driver: IMF flags EM sovereign spreads as vulnerable due to capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI, Fed hold, and dollar strength
spread_movements.asymmetric: BIS USD debt loop creates structural amplification: EM economies with high USD debt face rising refinancing costs as dollar strengthens, forcing further FX depreciation and sovereign stress. Structural loop, not cyclical.

spread_movements.driver: Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars over next 24 months plus 1 trillion dollars annual interest meets tariff-fueled inflation and Fed hold
spread_movements.asymmetric: Fiscal dominance risk rising: if Fed forced to hold or hike to combat inflation while Treasury rolls over 9.8 trillion dollars, risk of Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis rises materially. Non-consensus tail risk underweighted by markets focused on Iran war.

sovereign_debt.debt_sustainability_flags: The most acute sovereign debt sustainability concern this week is the intersection of the US Treasury maturity wall (9.8 trillion dollars over 24 months) and dual inflation vectors (tariffs plus 1 percentage point, energy shock). If the Fed is forced to hold or hike rates to combat inflation while the Treasury must roll over 9.8 trillion dollars, the risk of fiscal dominance (Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis) rises materially. Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide a cushion, but the cushion is thinning. This is a non-consensus tail risk with high impact if triggered. EM sovereign stress is the second acute concern: capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI, Fed hold, dollar strength, and the BIS USD debt loop create a structural refinancing crisis for EM economies with high USD-denominated debt.
sovereign_debt.imf_bis_signals: The IMF April 2026 WEO explicitly flags EM sovereign spreads as vulnerable and capital flows as increasingly skewed toward debt over FDI, raising refinancing risk. The IMF adverse scenario (growth 2.5 percent, inflation 5.4 percent) and severe scenario (growth 2.0 percent, inflation exceeding 6.0 percent) are plausible paths given Hormuz fragility, not tail risks. The BIS has flagged the USD debt loop in EM economies as a structural vulnerability amid rising dollar funding costs. No new FSB recommendations were active this week, but the IMF and BIS signals together point to a synchronized tightening of global financial conditions that disproportionately stresses EM sovereigns and raises fiscal dominance risk in advanced economies with high debt burdens.

threshold_assessments.title: IMF WEO April 2026: Global growth 3.1 percent, inflation 4.4 percent; Middle East war dominant shock
threshold_assessments.summary: The IMF downgraded global growth to 3.1 percent for 2026 and raised headline inflation to 4.4 percent, with the Middle East war identified as the dominant shock via a 19 percent energy price rise. Adverse scenario: growth 2.5 percent, inflation 5.4 percent. Severe scenario: growth 2.0 percent, inflation exceeding 6.0 percent. EM sovereign spreads flagged as vulnerable; capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI.
reliability_flags: Adverse and severe scenarios are plausible paths, not tail risks, given Hormuz fragility (5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal)
threshold_assessments.asymmetric: Consensus may be anchoring to the reference scenario (3.1 percent growth). GMM assessment: the adverse and severe scenarios are not tail risks but plausible paths. Market pricing may underweight the probability of the adverse scenario materializing. EM sovereign stress and fiscal dominance risk in advanced economies are underweighted structural implications.

threshold_assessments.title: PIIE: Section 301 tariff architecture post-SCOTUS IEEPA nullification
threshold_assessments.summary: PIIE analysis confirms USTR launched 76 Section 301 investigations (60 forced labor, 16 excess capacity) as a durable legal replacement for IEEPA-based tariffs struck down by the Supreme Court in February 2026. Section 301 is historically harder to challenge in court than IEEPA. BOP interim 10 percent tariff expires mid-July 2026; Section 301 outcomes will replace with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent. PIIE models tariff-driven inflation at plus 1 percentage point above baseline and growth drag at minus 0.62 percentage point in 2026.
reliability_flags: PIIE analysis by Alan Wolff, former WTO Deputy Director-General, provides high-confidence legal framing
threshold_assessments.asymmetric: Market pricing of bilateral framework agreements (UK, EU, Japan, Indonesia, Argentina, Ecuador) as de-escalation signals may be premature. The administration is constructing a permanent high-tariff architecture, not negotiating away leverage. Effective tariff rate baseline likely to rise post-BOP expiry, not fall. Divergence: market pricing de-escalation; GMM assesses structural entrenchment.

threshold_assessments.title: BIS: USD debt loop in EM economies as structural vulnerability
threshold_assessments.summary: The BIS has flagged the USD debt loop in EM economies as a structural vulnerability amid rising dollar funding costs. EM economies with high USD-denominated debt face rising refinancing costs as the dollar strengthens, which forces further FX depreciation, which raises import inflation and sovereign stress. This is a structural amplification mechanism, not a cyclical adjustment.
threshold_assessments.asymmetric: The BIS USD debt loop creates a structural refinancing crisis for EM economies. Combined with IMF flags on capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI and EM sovereign spreads as vulnerable, this points to a synchronized tightening of global financial conditions that disproportionately stresses EM sovereigns. Non-consensus structural risk.

research_highlights.title: PIIE Global Trade War Update: Tariff impacts on US growth and inflation
research_highlights.significance: PIIE modeling provides the quantitative basis for the Fed stagflation bind assessment: tariffs reduce US growth rate by 0.62 percentage point in 2026 and raise inflation by 1 percentage point above baseline. This dual constraint prevents both cuts (inflation) and hikes (growth drag), freezing the Fed in a stagflationary corridor.
research_highlights.asymmetric: Fed funds futures pricing 1 to 2 cuts in H2 2026 may be underpricing the persistence of tariff inflation and the structural nature of the Section 301 legal architecture. GMM assessment: bar for cuts is higher than market pricing implies; asymmetric risk toward extended hold or hike.

vectors.name: Policy Rate Divergence
vectors.headline: Fed and ECB both frozen by stagflation bind; divergence eliminated but stress elevated
vectors.summary: Fed holds at 3.5 to 3.75 percent, ECB holds all three key rates unchanged. Both central banks frozen by dual inflation and growth shocks. Easing cycles suspended indefinitely.
vectors.detail: The Fed and ECB are both in a stagflation bind: tariff-driven inflation (PIIE: plus 1 percentage point above baseline for US) and Middle East energy shock inflation prevent cuts, while tariff-induced growth drag (PIIE: minus 0.62 percentage point for US) and war-driven growth risk prevent hikes. Policy rate divergence has been eliminated, but the stress level is elevated because both central banks are frozen and unable to respond to either inflation or growth shocks. Market pricing of residual cuts in H2 2026 may be premature.
vectors.asymmetric: Consensus may interpret synchronized holds as reducing divergence risk. GMM assessment: synchronized holds under stagflation constraint represent elevated stress, not stability. Both central banks are frozen and unable to respond to shocks. Asymmetric risk toward extended hold or hike if inflation persists.

vectors.name: Sovereign Spread Widening
vectors.headline: EM sovereign spreads vulnerable; US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars meets tariff inflation
vectors.summary: IMF flags EM sovereign spreads as vulnerable due to capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI, Fed hold, and dollar strength. BIS USD debt loop amplifies EM refinancing stress. US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars over 24 months plus 1 trillion dollars annual interest meets dual inflation vectors.
vectors.detail: EM sovereign spreads are under acute stress from the intersection of Fed hold, dollar strength, IMF EM growth downgrade to 3.9 percent, and capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI. The BIS USD debt loop creates a structural amplification mechanism: EM economies with high USD-denominated debt face rising refinancing costs as the dollar strengthens, forcing further FX depreciation and sovereign stress. US sovereign stress is rising from the Treasury maturity wall (9.8 trillion dollars over 24 months) meeting dual inflation vectors (tariffs plus 1 percentage point, energy shock). Fiscal dominance risk rising: if Fed forced to hold or hike to combat inflation while Treasury rolls over 9.8 trillion dollars, risk of Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis rises materially.
vectors.asymmetric: Consensus focus on Middle East war and energy shock. GMM assessment: the intersection of EM USD debt loop, US Treasury maturity wall, and dual inflation vectors creates a fiscal dominance scenario that is structurally more dangerous than a transient energy shock. Non-consensus tail risk with high impact if triggered.

vectors.name: Currency Regime Stress
vectors.headline: EM FX under pressure from Fed hold, dollar strength, and EM growth downgrade; gold accumulation continues
vectors.summary: EM FX depreciation driven by Fed hold, dollar strength, IMF EM growth downgrade to 3.9 percent, and capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI. Gold reserve ratio in EM central banks rising as dollar weaponization concerns persist.
vectors.detail: EM FX is under acute stress from the intersection of Fed hold at 3.5 to 3.75 percent, dollar safe-haven bid, IMF EM growth downgrade to 3.9 percent, and capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI. The BIS USD debt loop amplifies EM FX stress: EM economies with high USD-denominated debt face rising refinancing costs as the dollar strengthens, which forces further FX depreciation, which raises import inflation and sovereign stress. This is a structural loop, not a cyclical adjustment. EM central banks continue to accumulate gold reserves as dollar weaponization concerns persist, representing a structural shift away from USD reserves.
vectors.asymmetric: Market may be underpricing the duration and severity of EM FX stress given the structural nature of the BIS USD debt loop and the Fed stagflation bind. Dollar strength is not a transient safe-haven bid but a structural outcome of the tariff regime and energy shock. EM FX depreciation is a structural adjustment, not a cyclical correction.

vectors.name: Private Credit Cascade
vectors.headline: Private credit gate events flagged; real M2 negative post-CPI 3.3 percent hits liquidity
vectors.summary: Reasoner flags private credit gate events and real M2 negative as structural liquidity deterioration masked by nominal M2. Blind Spot 2 active.
vectors.detail: Private credit markets face gate events (investor redemption restrictions) as real M2 turns negative post-CPI 3.3 percent, representing structural liquidity deterioration masked by nominal M2 growth. The reasoner flags this as Blind Spot 2 (Nominal M2 Mirage): nominal M2 growth around 1.8 to 2.0 percent year-over-year appears supportive, but real M2 is negative after adjusting for CPI 3.3 percent, confirming risk-off for Crypto and Tech asset classes. Private credit cascade risk is elevated but not yet high, as gate events remain isolated and have not triggered broader NBFI stress.
vectors.asymmetric: Consensus may be anchoring to nominal M2 growth as a liquidity support signal. GMM assessment: real M2 negative represents structural liquidity tightening that raises private credit cascade risk. Gate events are early warning signals, not isolated incidents. Asymmetric risk toward broader NBFI stress if liquidity tightening persists.

vectors.name: Liquidity Fragmentation
vectors.headline: Treasury market liquidity WARNING active; bank reserves 3.2 trillion dollars cushion thinning
vectors.summary: Treasury market liquidity WARNING active with 30-year auction tail and elevated bid-offer spreads. Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide cushion, but cushion thinning amid Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars.
vectors.detail: Treasury market liquidity remains under stress, with the WARNING flag active due to 30-year auction tail and elevated bid-offer spreads in on-the-run Treasuries. Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide a liquidity cushion, but the cushion is thinning as the Treasury faces a 9.8 trillion dollar maturity wall over the next 24 months. If Treasury issuance accelerates to meet the maturity wall while the Fed holds rates to combat inflation, liquidity fragmentation risk rises materially. FX swap basis partial normalization on ceasefire news is episodic (TS noise), not structural (SA movement); volumes remain at all-time high 3.6 trillion dollars notional.
vectors.asymmetric: Consensus may interpret bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars as ample liquidity. GMM assessment: reserves are ample but on a declining trajectory, and the Treasury maturity wall creates a structural demand for liquidity that could fragment markets if the Fed is unable to ease. Asymmetric risk toward liquidity fragmentation if Treasury issuance accelerates.

vectors.name: Commodity Price Transmission
vectors.headline: Middle East war drives 19 percent energy price rise; Brent 104 dollars approaching contraction threshold
vectors.summary: IMF flags Middle East war as dominant shock via 19 percent energy price rise. Brent crude at 104 dollars approaching Oxford Economics contraction threshold. Hormuz transit 5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal.
vectors.detail: The Middle East war has driven a 19 percent energy price rise in the IMF reference scenario, with Brent crude at 104 dollars approaching the Oxford Economics contraction threshold. Hormuz Strait transit remains fragile at 5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal, despite a fragile ceasefire. The IMF adverse scenario (further disruption) projects energy prices rising further, pushing inflation to 5.4 percent and growth falling to 2.5 percent. The severe scenario (energy disruption extending into 2027) projects inflation exceeding 6.0 percent and growth at 2.0 percent. Commodity price transmission is a primary CRISIS driver, with energy shock adding a second inflationary vector alongside tariffs.
vectors.asymmetric: Consensus may be anchoring to the IMF reference scenario (19 percent energy price rise). GMM assessment: the adverse and severe scenarios are plausible paths, not tail risks, given Hormuz fragility and no credible ceasefire mechanism. Market pricing may underweight the probability of the adverse scenario materializing. Energy shock is not transient but structural until Hormuz transit normalizes.

vectors.name: Banking Sector Stress
vectors.headline: CRE fragility phase intact; no new acute stress signals this week
vectors.summary: CRE fragility phase continues with elevated delinquency rates, but no new acute stress signals this week. Banking sector stress remains moderate.
vectors.detail: Commercial real estate (CRE) fragility continues with elevated CMBS office delinquency rates, but no new acute stress signals emerged this week. The Fed hold at 3.5 to 3.75 percent and 10-year Treasury yield at 4.35 percent sustain CRE refinancing pressure, but the stress remains contained within the CRE segment and has not triggered broader banking sector contagion. G-SIB capital requirements remain stable, and CDS spreads for major G-SIBs have not widened materially. Banking sector stress is moderate, not elevated.
vectors.asymmetric: Consensus may be underpricing the duration of CRE stress given the Fed stagflation bind and the likelihood of extended hold or hike. GMM assessment: CRE fragility is a slow-burn stress that could escalate if the Fed is forced to hike to combat inflation, but the risk is not acute this week. Asymmetric risk toward escalation if Fed hikes.

vectors.name: Trade Policy Shock
vectors.headline: USTR Section 301 legal architecture shift creates durable high-tariff regime; BOP expiry mid-July 2026
vectors.summary: USTR launches 76 Section 301 investigations as durable legal replacement for IEEPA-based tariffs. BOP interim 10 percent tariff expires mid-July 2026; Section 301 outcomes will replace with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent.
vectors.detail: The USTR Section 301 investigations represent a regime-level legal architecture shift to a more challenge-resistant statutory basis for tariffs. The BOP interim 10 percent tariff (Section 122) expires mid-July 2026, creating a hard policy deadline. Section 301 outcomes will replace the BOP tariff with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent in targeted areas (forced labor, excess capacity). The effective tariff rate baseline of 11.8 percent is likely to rise post-BOP expiry, not fall. Market pricing of bilateral framework agreements (UK, EU, Japan, Indonesia, Argentina, Ecuador) as de-escalation signals may be premature — the administration is constructing a permanent high-tariff architecture, not negotiating away leverage. Trade policy shock is a primary CRISIS driver affecting six asset classes directly.
vectors.asymmetric: Consensus narrative: bilateral deals signal tariff de-escalation path. GMM assessment: Section 301 architecture signals tariff leverage as permanent structural tool. Effective tariff rate baseline likely to rise post-BOP expiry, not fall. Divergence: market pricing de-escalation; GMM assesses structural entrenchment. Non-consensus structural risk.

items.title: Treasury market liquidity WARNING active; 30-year auction tail and elevated bid-offer spreads
items.summary: Treasury market liquidity remains under stress, with the WARNING flag active due to 30-year auction tail and elevated bid-offer spreads in on-the-run Treasuries. Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide a liquidity cushion, but the cushion is thinning as the Treasury faces a 9.8 trillion dollar maturity wall over the next 24 months.
items.asymmetric: If Treasury issuance accelerates to meet the maturity wall while the Fed holds rates to combat inflation, liquidity fragmentation risk rises materially. Fiscal dominance risk: if Fed forced to hold or hike to combat inflation while Treasury rolls over 9.8 trillion dollars, risk of Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis rises materially. Non-consensus tail risk.

items.title: Private credit gate events flagged; real M2 negative post-CPI 3.3 percent
items.summary: Private credit markets face gate events (investor redemption restrictions) as real M2 turns negative post-CPI 3.3 percent, representing structural liquidity deterioration masked by nominal M2 growth. Blind Spot 2 (Nominal M2 Mirage) active: nominal M2 growth around 1.8 to 2.0 percent year-over-year appears supportive, but real M2 is negative after adjusting for CPI 3.3 percent.
items.asymmetric: Private credit cascade risk is elevated but not yet high, as gate events remain isolated and have not triggered broader NBFI stress. Asymmetric risk toward broader NBFI stress if liquidity tightening persists and gate events spread.

items.title: FX swap basis partial normalization episodic; volumes remain at all-time high 3.6 trillion dollars notional
items.summary: FX swap basis partial normalization on ceasefire news is episodic (TS noise), not structural (SA movement). Volumes remain at all-time high 3.6 trillion dollars notional, indicating structural dollar funding stress persists despite tactical easing.
items.asymmetric: Reasoner flags fx swap basis normalization as TS episodic noise, not SA movement. Structural dollar funding stress persists. Market may be overinterpreting ceasefire-driven normalization as a structural easing signal.

module_9.title: Trade and Tariff Policy
module_9.digest_note: Section 301 legal architecture shift is the dominant trade policy development this week — a regime-level structural change, not a tactical escalation.

new_developments.title: USTR launches 76 Section 301 investigations as durable legal replacement for IEEPA-based tariffs
new_developments.actors: United States — dozens of economies (forced labor and excess capacity targets)
new_developments.summary: The USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations — 60 for inadequate foreign enforcement against forced labor and 16 for structural excess manufacturing capacity — as a permanent legal replacement for IEEPA-based tariffs struck down by the Supreme Court in February 2026. Section 301 is a statutory trade retaliation authority that courts have historically been reluctant to override, making this a structural hardening of the tariff regime. The BOP interim 10 percent blanket tariff (Section 122) serves as a bridge pending Section 301 outcomes, with the 150-day BOP clock expiring mid-July 2026.
new_developments.enforcement: Section 301 investigations underway; outcomes expected to replace BOP tariff with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent in targeted areas by mid-July 2026.
new_developments.asymmetric: Market pricing of bilateral framework agreements (UK, EU, Japan, Indonesia, Argentina, Ecuador) as de-escalation signals may be premature. The administration is constructing a permanent high-tariff architecture, not negotiating away leverage. Effective tariff rate baseline likely to rise post-BOP expiry, not fall. Divergence: market pricing de-escalation; GMM assesses structural entrenchment.

new_developments.title: EU-US Turnberry tariff deal locks in 15 percent rate on EU exports
new_developments.summary: The Turnberry deal fixes US tariffs on EU exports at 15 percent, reducing uncertainty but eroding EU competitiveness as tariffs on rivals like China converge to similar levels. EU export growth to US projected to slow 4.6 percent in 2026 due to higher effective rates and relative disadvantage. This shifts trade drag from uncertainty to structural competitiveness loss.
new_developments.enforcement: Pending national ratification; European Parliament endorsed deal but implementation status unclear.
new_developments.asymmetric: Solidifies tariff shock as competitiveness drag rather than episodic uncertainty, upgrading EU-US trade friction to cyclical headwind. Market may be overinterpreting Turnberry deal as de-escalation when it actually locks in a high-tariff baseline.

new_developments.title: USTR 2026 Trade Policy Agenda doubles down on America First tariffs
new_developments.summary: USTR 2026 Trade Policy Agenda emphasizes enforcing existing tariffs, monitoring ART commitments, and USMCA review to address worker impacts. Highlights trade deficit shift with EU surpassing China. Signals sustained high-tariff regime without de-escalation.
new_developments.enforcement: Active enforcement of existing tariffs; USMCA Joint Review underway.
new_developments.asymmetric: Confirms tariff shock as entrenched structural policy. Market pricing of de-escalation path may be premature given explicit USTR commitment to doubling down on America First tariffs.

no_change.item: EU steel and aluminum face sustained 25 percent and 10 percent US Section 232 tariffs
no_change.status: Ongoing Section 232 tariffs at 25 percent on steel, 10 percent on aluminum create high costs for EU auto, machinery, construction exporters. Part of overlapping regimes with no resolution in sight despite negotiations.
no_change.next_trigger: Negotiation progress on Section 232 or EU retaliation escalation.

module_9.friction_analysis: Trade friction is highest this week in the US-China and US-EU corridors, driven by the USTR Section 301 legal architecture shift. The 76 Section 301 investigations target forced labor (60 investigations) and excess capacity (16 investigations) across dozens of economies, with China and EU exporters likely to be primary targets. The BOP interim 10 percent tariff expires mid-July 2026, creating a hard policy deadline for Section 301 outcomes. The Turnberry deal locks in a 15 percent tariff rate on EU exports, shifting trade drag from uncertainty to structural competitiveness loss. The USTR 2026 Trade Policy Agenda signals sustained high-tariff regime without de-escalation, with USMCA review adding pressure on Mexico and Canada. Trade friction is structural, not cyclical, and likely to intensify post-BOP expiry.
module_9.tariff_escalation_rung: The tariff escalation rung remains at T3 (Enacted tariffs with active retaliation) but is structurally hardening toward T4 (Broad-based tariffs with supply chain disruption) as the Section 301 legal architecture takes effect. The BOP interim 10 percent tariff (T3) expires mid-July 2026, and Section 301 outcomes will replace it with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent in targeted areas (forced labor, excess capacity). The effective tariff rate baseline of 11.8 percent is likely to rise post-BOP expiry, not fall. The critical path is the mid-July 2026 BOP expiry deadline: if Section 301 investigations produce tariffs exceeding 15 percent in multiple sectors, the rung escalates to T4. Market pricing of de-escalation is premature; GMM assesses structural entrenchment and rising effective tariff baseline.

retaliation_chain.measure: Retaliatory tariffs on US agricultural products
retaliation_chain.macro_impact: EU retaliation sustains trade friction and raises input costs for US exporters, but impact is moderate relative to US tariffs on EU exports.

retaliation_chain.measure: Retaliatory tariffs on US agricultural and industrial goods
retaliation_chain.macro_impact: China retaliation likely to escalate post-Section 301 outcomes given forced labor and excess capacity targeting. Macro impact high if retaliation extends to rare earth exports or technology transfer restrictions.

multilateral_signals.item: IMF April 2026 WEO downgrades global growth to 3.1 percent, raises inflation to 4.4 percent; flags EM sovereign spreads as vulnerable
multilateral_signals.summary: The IMF April 2026 World Economic Outlook represents a material downgrade of global growth and upgrade of inflation, with the Middle East war identified as the dominant shock. The IMF explicitly flags EM sovereign spreads as vulnerable and capital flows as increasingly skewed toward debt over FDI, raising refinancing risk. The adverse and severe scenarios are plausible paths, not tail risks, given Hormuz fragility.
multilateral_signals.asymmetric: The IMF assessment confirms the CRISIS regime and provides multilateral institutional validation of the stagflation constraint binding both the Fed and ECB. The IMF adverse scenario (growth 2.5 percent, inflation 5.4 percent) is a plausible path that markets may be underweighting. EM sovereign stress and fiscal dominance risk in advanced economies are underweighted structural implications.

multilateral_signals.item: BIS flags USD debt loop in EM economies as structural vulnerability amid rising dollar funding costs
multilateral_signals.summary: The BIS has flagged the USD debt loop in EM economies as a structural vulnerability. EM economies with high USD-denominated debt face rising refinancing costs as the dollar strengthens, which forces further FX depreciation, which raises import inflation and sovereign stress. This is a structural amplification mechanism, not a cyclical adjustment.
multilateral_signals.asymmetric: The BIS USD debt loop creates a structural refinancing crisis for EM economies. Combined with IMF flags on capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI and EM sovereign spreads as vulnerable, this points to a synchronized tightening of global financial conditions that disproportionately stresses EM sovereigns. Non-consensus structural risk underweighted by markets.

central_bank_coordination.item: Fed and ECB both frozen by stagflation bind; policy rate divergence eliminated but stress elevated
central_bank_coordination.summary: The Fed holds at 3.5 to 3.75 percent, ECB holds all three key rates unchanged. Both central banks are frozen by dual inflation and growth shocks. Easing cycles suspended indefinitely. Policy rate divergence has been eliminated, but the stress level is elevated because both central banks are frozen and unable to respond to either inflation or growth shocks.

fiscal_governance.item: US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars over 24 months plus 1 trillion dollars annual interest meets dual inflation vectors
fiscal_governance.summary: The US faces a 9.8 trillion dollar Treasury maturity wall over the next 24 months, with annual interest expense exceeding 1 trillion dollars. Tariff-driven inflation (PIIE: plus 1 percentage point above baseline) and Middle East energy shock inflation create a dual vector that constrains Fed easing and raises refinancing costs. Fiscal dominance risk rising: if Fed forced to hold or hike to combat inflation while Treasury rolls over 9.8 trillion dollars, risk of Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis rises materially.

governance_gaps.gap: No multilateral mechanism to resolve Section 301 tariff architecture or coordinate de-escalation
governance_gaps.analysis: The USTR Section 301 legal architecture shift creates a durable high-tariff regime that is harder to challenge in court than IEEPA-based tariffs. There is no multilateral mechanism (WTO, G7, G20) to coordinate de-escalation or resolve the structural tariff baseline. Bilateral framework agreements (UK, EU, Japan, Indonesia, Argentina, Ecuador) are tactical accommodations, not strategic de-escalation. The absence of a multilateral governance mechanism means the tariff regime is likely to persist and intensify.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: If the Section 301 investigations produce tariffs exceeding 15 percent in multiple sectors and no multilateral de-escalation mechanism emerges, the effective tariff rate baseline will rise structurally, not fall. This is a governance gap with high macro impact: sustained high tariffs reduce global trade, raise inflation, and lower growth. Non-consensus structural risk.

governance_gaps.gap: No coordinated central bank response to dual inflation and growth shocks; Fed and ECB both frozen
governance_gaps.analysis: The Fed and ECB are both frozen by stagflation bind, with no coordinated central bank response to dual inflation and growth shocks. The absence of central bank coordination means monetary policy is unable to respond to either inflation or growth shocks, raising the risk of fiscal dominance (central banks forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crises) or prolonged stagflation.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: If the Middle East war persists and energy prices remain elevated, and if Section 301 tariffs produce a second inflation wave, the Fed and ECB will remain frozen indefinitely. The absence of coordinated central bank response is a governance gap with high macro impact: prolonged stagflation or fiscal dominance. Non-consensus structural risk.

module_11.title: Scenario and Tail Risk

items.title: Section 301 tariff pipeline creates durable greater than 15 percent effective rate post-BOP expiry; market mispricing as de-escalation
items.probability_delta: Probability rising: BOP expiry mid-July 2026 creates hard policy deadline; Section 301 investigations targeting 76 economies likely to produce sector-specific tariffs exceeding 10 percent in forced labor and excess capacity areas. Market pricing de-escalation; GMM assesses structural entrenchment.
items.asymmetric: If Section 301 investigations produce tariffs exceeding 15 percent in multiple sectors (forced labor, excess capacity) and the effective tariff rate baseline rises above 15 percent post-BOP expiry, the tariff escalation rung moves to T4 (Broad-based tariffs with supply chain disruption). This affects Metals, Tech, EM Equities, and Consumer Staples most acutely. Pharma 100 percent tariff in September 2026 adds a second vector. Non-consensus tail risk with high impact.

items.title: US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars plus 1 trillion dollars interest meets tariff-fueled inflation; forces fiscal dominance
items.probability_delta: Probability rising: dual inflation vectors (tariffs plus 1 percentage point, energy shock) constrain Fed easing while Treasury must roll over 9.8 trillion dollars. Bank reserves 3.2 trillion dollars cushion thinning. If Fed forced to hold or hike to combat inflation, refinancing costs rise materially, raising fiscal dominance risk.
items.asymmetric: If the Fed is forced to hold or hike rates to combat inflation while the Treasury rolls over 9.8 trillion dollars, the risk of fiscal dominance (Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis) rises materially. This is a non-consensus tail risk underweighted by markets focused on the Iran war. Impact is systemic: fiscal dominance breaks Fed independence and triggers sovereign debt crisis. Probability 0.65, impact 0.90.

items.title: IMF adverse scenario (growth 2.5 percent, inflation 5.4 percent) materializes if Middle East war persists and Hormuz transit remains fragile
items.probability_delta: Probability rising: Hormuz transit remains at 5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal despite fragile ceasefire. No credible ceasefire mechanism. If war persists and energy disruption extends, IMF adverse scenario (growth 2.5 percent, inflation 5.4 percent) is plausible path, not tail risk.
items.asymmetric: Consensus may be anchoring to the IMF reference scenario (growth 3.1 percent, inflation 4.4 percent). GMM assessment: the adverse scenario is a plausible path given Hormuz fragility and no credible ceasefire mechanism. Market pricing may underweight the probability of the adverse scenario materializing. If adverse scenario materializes, EM sovereign spreads widen materially, Fed and ECB remain frozen, and fiscal dominance risk rises. High impact.

structural_trends.title: Dollar weaponization and EM central bank gold accumulation
structural_trends.trend: EM central banks continue to accumulate gold reserves as dollar weaponization concerns persist, representing a structural shift away from USD reserves. Gold reserve ratio in EM central banks rising. This is a multi-year structural trend, not a cyclical adjustment.

structural_trends.title: Tariff regime as permanent structural tool, not negotiating chip
structural_trends.trend: The USTR Section 301 legal architecture shift signals the administration intends to maintain tariff leverage as a permanent structural tool, not a negotiating chip to be retired post-bilateral framework agreements. Effective tariff rate baseline likely to rise post-BOP expiry, not fall. This is a regime-level structural trend.

structural_trends.title: Capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI in EM economies
structural_trends.trend: IMF flags capital flows as increasingly skewed toward debt over FDI in EM economies, raising refinancing risk. This is a structural shift in EM financing patterns that increases sovereign vulnerability to Fed hold and dollar strength. Multi-year structural trend.

asymmetric_flags: Market pricing of bilateral framework agreements (UK, EU, Japan, Indonesia, Argentina, Ecuador) as de-escalation signals may be premature — the administration is constructing a permanent high-tariff architecture, not negotiating away leverage.
Consensus may be anchoring to the IMF reference scenario (growth 3.1 percent). GMM assessment: the adverse and severe scenarios are plausible paths, not tail risks, given Hormuz fragility.
Fed funds futures pricing 1 to 2 cuts in H2 2026 may be underpricing the persistence of tariff inflation and the structural nature of the Section 301 legal architecture.

Consensus focus on Middle East war and energy shock. GMM assessment: the intersection of EM USD debt loop, US Treasury maturity wall, and dual inflation vectors creates a fiscal dominance scenario that is structurally more dangerous than a transient energy shock.

Bilateral framework agreement with US provides tactical relief; BoE policy stance stable; external vulnerability low relative to EM

central_bank_scorecard.forward_guidance: No forward guidance shift; stagflation constraint binding
central_bank_scorecard.key_development: Fed frozen by dual inflation and growth shocks; tariff inflation prevents cuts, growth drag prevents hikes; easing cycle suspended indefinitely

central_bank_scorecard.forward_guidance: Upside inflation risks and downside growth risks have intensified; easing cycle suspended indefinitely

central_bank_scorecard.last_action: No new action reported this week
central_bank_scorecard.forward_guidance: Easing bias to support growth amid external headwinds

central_bank_scorecard.last_action: No new action reported this week

macro_health_composite.note: Macro health composite deteriorating driven by stagflation bind (growth and inflation shocks simultaneous), EM sovereign stress, and tariff regime structural hardening. Growth stability low (IMF 3.1 percent, adverse 2.5 percent plausible). Inflation anchor weak (Fed and ECB frozen, dual inflation vectors). Financial stability moderate (Treasury liquidity WARNING, private credit gates, but no acute systemic stress). External balance weak (EM FX under pressure, BIS USD debt loop). Policy coherence weak (no multilateral de-escalation mechanism, Fed and ECB both frozen).

key_judgments.judgment: The USTR Section 301 legal architecture shift represents a regime-level structural hardening of the tariff regime, not a tactical escalation. The effective tariff rate baseline is likely to rise post-BOP expiry (mid-July 2026), not fall, as Section 301 outcomes replace the BOP interim 10 percent tariff with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent in targeted areas (forced labor, excess capacity). Market pricing of bilateral framework agreements as de-escalation signals is premature.

The Fed and ECB are both frozen by a stagflation bind: tariff-driven inflation and Middle East energy shock inflation prevent cuts, while tariff-induced growth drag and war-driven growth risk prevent hikes. Easing cycles are suspended indefinitely. Market pricing of Fed cuts in H2 2026 is premature given the dual inflation vectors and the structural nature of the Section 301 tariff architecture. Asymmetric risk is toward extended hold or hike if inflation persists.

The IMF adverse scenario (global growth 2.5 percent, inflation 5.4 percent) is a plausible path, not a tail risk, given Hormuz fragility (5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal) and no credible ceasefire mechanism. Consensus may be anchoring to the reference scenario (growth 3.1 percent, inflation 4.4 percent). Market pricing may underweight the probability of the adverse scenario materializing. If the adverse scenario materializes, EM sovereign spreads widen materially, Fed and ECB remain frozen, and fiscal dominance risk rises.

The intersection of the US Treasury maturity wall (9.8 trillion dollars over 24 months) and dual inflation vectors (tariffs plus 1 percentage point, energy shock) creates a fiscal dominance risk that is structurally more dangerous than a transient energy shock. If the Fed is forced to hold or hike rates to combat inflation while the Treasury rolls over 9.8 trillion dollars, the risk of fiscal dominance (Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis) rises materially. This is a non-consensus tail risk underweighted by markets focused on the Iran war.

EM sovereign spreads are under acute stress from the intersection of Fed hold, dollar strength, IMF EM growth downgrade to 3.9 percent, and capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI. The BIS USD debt loop creates a structural amplification mechanism: EM economies with high USD-denominated debt face rising refinancing costs as the dollar strengthens, forcing further FX depreciation and sovereign stress. This is a structural loop, not a cyclical adjustment. EM sovereign stress is a primary CRISIS driver.

gaps_register.description: Section 301 investigation outcomes and sector-specific tariff rates are not yet published. Evidence of final tariff rates in forced labor and excess capacity sectors would upgrade claim gmm-int-2026-05-15-0007 from Assessed to High or Confirmed.
Turnberry deal national ratification status is unclear. Evidence of EU member state ratification progress would upgrade claim gmm-int-2026-05-15-0021 confidence and clarify enforcement timeline.

Private credit gate event scope and NBFI contagion risk are not quantified. Evidence of gate event spread across private credit funds or NBFI stress indicators would upgrade module_8 private credit cascade assessment from Assessed to High.

Middle East ceasefire durability and Hormuz transit normalization timeline are uncertain. Evidence of sustained Hormuz transit recovery or ceasefire breakdown would materially affect IMF adverse scenario probability assessment in key_judgments kj-003.

cross_monitor_candidates.signal: USTR Section 301 legal architecture shift creates durable high-tariff regime affecting EU exporters; Turnberry deal locks in 15 percent tariff baseline; EU export growth to US projected to slow 4.6 percent in 2026

EM sovereign spreads vulnerable; BIS USD debt loop amplifies refinancing stress; capital flows skewed toward debt over FDI; EM growth downgraded to 3.9 percent
Private credit gate events flagged; real M2 negative post-CPI 3.3 percent; AI capex resilience questioned amid tariff supply chain stress and liquidity tightening

conviction_history.rationale: System average -0.686 with all 8 asset classes BEARISH or MILD NEGATIVE; three SA corroborators (tariffs, energy shock, fiscal stress) exceed regime change threshold; no contradicting SA signals.

System average stable at -0.686; Section 301 legal architecture shift adds durable tariff vector; Fed and ECB stagflation bind confirmed; IMF adverse scenario plausible path; EM sovereign stress and US fiscal dominance risk rising.

blind_spots.name: Earnings Suppression
blind_spots.description: Q1 pre-tariff earnings beats mask Q2 guidance risk as Section 301 investigations escalate supply chain costs. EPS revision breadth minus 15 percent confirms distortion. Tech sector bifurcated: AI capex resilience versus tariff supply chain stress.

blind_spots.name: Nominal M2 Mirage
blind_spots.description: Nominal M2 growth around 1.8 to 2.0 percent year-over-year appears supportive, but real M2 is negative after adjusting for CPI 3.3 percent. This masks structural liquidity deterioration and confirms risk-off for Crypto and Tech asset classes.

active_tactical_alerts.name: BOP Tariff Expiry Mid-July 2026
active_tactical_alerts.description: Balance of Payments interim 10 percent tariff expires mid-July 2026. Section 301 investigations underway to replace BOP tariff with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent in forced labor and excess capacity areas. Hard policy deadline creates market repricing risk if Section 301 outcomes exceed market expectations.

active_tactical_alerts.name: IMF Adverse Scenario Probability Rising
active_tactical_alerts.description: IMF adverse scenario (global growth 2.5 percent, inflation 5.4 percent) is plausible path given Hormuz fragility (5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal) and no credible ceasefire mechanism. Market may be underweighting probability of adverse scenario materializing. If Middle East war persists and energy disruption extends, adverse scenario becomes base case.

active_tactical_alerts.name: US Fiscal Dominance Risk
active_tactical_alerts.description: US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars over 24 months plus 1 trillion dollars annual interest meets dual inflation vectors (tariffs plus 1 percentage point, energy shock). If Fed forced to hold or hike to combat inflation while Treasury rolls over 9.8 trillion dollars, risk of fiscal dominance (Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis) rises materially. Non-consensus tail risk with high impact if triggered.

open_findings.headline: Private credit gate events flagged; real M2 negative post-CPI 3.3 percent masks structural liquidity deterioration
open_findings.why_it_matters: Nominal M2 growth appears supportive but real M2 negative confirms risk-off for Crypto and Tech; private credit cascade risk elevated

open_findings.headline: Middle East war drives 19 percent energy price rise; Brent crude 104 dollars approaching contraction threshold; Hormuz transit 5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal
open_findings.why_it_matters: Energy shock is primary CRISIS driver; IMF adverse scenario (further disruption) and severe scenario (energy disruption extending into 2027) are plausible paths, not tail risks

proposed_patches.rule_applied: Risk vector rating change based on new SA evidence
proposed_patches.material_change_justification: USTR Section 301 legal architecture shift represents regime-level structural hardening of tariff regime; effective tariff rate baseline likely to rise post-BOP expiry, not fall; affects six asset classes directly
proposed_patches.rationale: Section 301 investigations create durable high-tariff architecture; BOP expiry mid-July 2026 creates hard policy deadline; market mispricing de-escalation

proposed_patches.rule_applied: Domain stress level escalation based on central bank policy constraint
proposed_patches.material_change_justification: Fed and ECB both frozen by stagflation bind; easing cycles suspended indefinitely; dual inflation and growth shocks create regime-level policy constraint
proposed_patches.rationale: Central banks unable to respond to either inflation or growth shocks; asymmetric risk toward extended hold or hike

proposed_patches.rule_applied: Domain stress level escalation based on EM sovereign spreads and US fiscal dominance risk
proposed_patches.material_change_justification: EM sovereign spreads vulnerable; BIS USD debt loop amplifies refinancing stress; US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars meets dual inflation vectors; fiscal dominance risk rising
proposed_patches.rationale: Dual sovereign stress vectors: EM refinancing crisis and US fiscal dominance risk; both rising materially this week

proposed_patches.rule_applied: Append new conviction history entry per Rule 28
proposed_patches.material_change_justification: System average stable at -0.686; Section 301 legal architecture shift adds durable tariff vector; Fed and ECB stagflation bind confirmed; IMF adverse scenario plausible path

new_value.rationale: System average stable at -0.686; Section 301 legal architecture shift adds durable tariff vector; Fed and ECB stagflation bind confirmed; IMF adverse scenario plausible path; EM sovereign stress and US fiscal dominance risk rising.

proposed_patches.rationale: Weekly conviction assessment appended per Rule 28; no change in system average or regime conviction from prior cycle

proposed_patches.rule_applied: Update active tactical alerts per Rule 30
proposed_patches.material_change_justification: BOP tariff expiry mid-July 2026 creates hard policy deadline; IMF adverse scenario probability rising given Hormuz fragility; US fiscal dominance risk rising from maturity wall and dual inflation vectors

new_value.name: BOP Tariff Expiry Mid-July 2026
new_value.description: Balance of Payments interim 10 percent tariff expires mid-July 2026. Section 301 investigations underway to replace BOP tariff with sector-specific tariffs likely exceeding 10 percent in forced labor and excess capacity areas. Hard policy deadline creates market repricing risk if Section 301 outcomes exceed market expectations.

new_value.name: IMF Adverse Scenario Probability Rising
new_value.description: IMF adverse scenario (global growth 2.5 percent, inflation 5.4 percent) is plausible path given Hormuz fragility (5 to 10 ships per day versus 138 normal) and no credible ceasefire mechanism. Market may be underweighting probability of adverse scenario materializing. If Middle East war persists and energy disruption extends, adverse scenario becomes base case.

new_value.name: US Fiscal Dominance Risk
new_value.description: US Treasury maturity wall 9.8 trillion dollars over 24 months plus 1 trillion dollars annual interest meets dual inflation vectors (tariffs plus 1 percentage point, energy shock). If Fed forced to hold or hike to combat inflation while Treasury rolls over 9.8 trillion dollars, risk of fiscal dominance (Fed forced to ease to prevent sovereign debt crisis) rises materially. Non-consensus tail risk with high impact if triggered.

proposed_patches.rationale: Three tactical alerts active: BOP expiry WARNING, IMF adverse scenario WATCH, US fiscal dominance WATCH; all state changes this cycle
